We believe that collaboration is key to unlocking innovation. By joining forces with our customers and partners, we can collectively shape the future of SAP’s products and change the way businesses operate.

So, mark your calendars, gather your team, and let's embark on this journey together. Together, we can drive progress, transform industries, and create a brighter future.

Don't delay – register today on influence.sap.com and be a part of the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Cycle 2 in 2024!

Warm regards,

Your SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Team

Follow-up after registration: When you register for a project, you will be invited to an introductory call with the SAP project lead. At this point further participation is optional. Typically, all activities are governed by the Feedback Agreement with SAP.

entry.title: Join Customer Engagement Intiative for Embedded Virtual Card Payments for SAP Ariba Procurement
entry.content:
Virtual credit card payments for Vendors/Suppliers using SAP Ariba and SAP Taulia can help stretch your Working Capital by providing "FREE" credit for upto 60 days - the time between card charge and paying the Bank after card statement, and earn cash rebates reducing the cost of the purchase. Virtual card payments are better alternative to Procurement Cards - since it is single use, specific to a Supplier, specific to PO or Invoice, has a maximum amount specified to be charged and offers other payment controls to make transaction secure which can be easily reconciled with Accounts Payable (AP) transaction records.

Help us build the right product for making Virtual Credit Card payments from SAP Ariba, by joining (registering) this Customer Engagement Initiative: Embedded Virtual Card Payments for SAP Ariba Procurement and SAP Business Network using Taulia (https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#campaign/3707)

Virtual cards are becoming increasingly essential in procurement applications like SAP Ariba. Virtual cards are revolutionizing B2B payments by offering a secure, efficient, and transparent alternative to traditional methods like checks or physical cards. Virtual cards are digitally generated, single-use or limited-use credit card numbers used for specific B2B transactions. Like physical cards, they have 16-digit numbers, expiration dates, and CVVs, but exist only online. Businesses can generate virtual cards through online platforms provided by financial institutions or fintech companies. They can set predefined spending limits, vendor restrictions, tie to particular purchase order(s) or invoice(s) or purchasing transaction(s), set activation and expiration dates for each card, ensuring tight control over expenses. Once created, the card details can be used for online payments or through mobile wallets for in-person transactions. Integrating virtual card functionality into procurement applications like SAP Ariba offers a multitude of benefits for both buyers and suppliers, streamlining the procurement process, enhancing security, and boosting overall efficiency.

Depending on the source, there are varying statistics regarding B2B virtual card usage. RFI / Visa study says the global value of virtual card transactions is expected to increase from $1.9 trillion in 2021 to an astronomical $6.8 trillion by 2026 and the global B2B virtual card market is expected to reach a staggering USD 1,291.05 billion (or USD $1.3 Trillion) by 2030, growing at a CAGR of 21.15% (Straits Research).

Benefits for B2B buyers:

  • Enhanced security: Eliminates risks associated with physical cards, like loss, theft, or data breaches. Each transaction generates a unique number, minimizing fraud exposure.

  • Improved payment control: Predefined spending limits and vendor restrictions ensure compliance with company policies and prevent unauthorized purchases.

  • Streamlined reconciliation: Real-time transaction data simplifies expense tracking and reconciliation, saving time and resources.

  • Faster payments: Virtual cards facilitate instant payments, improving supplier relationships and potentially earning early payment discounts.

  • Increased transparency: Detailed transaction data provides valuable insights into spending patterns, helping optimize budgets and negotiate better deals with vendors.

Benefits for B2B sellers:

  • Guaranteed payment: Reduces the risk of bounced checks or delayed payments.

  • Faster transaction processing: Streamlined payment process leads to quicker funds receipt.

  • Simplified reconciliation: Virtual cards provide consistent data format for easier reconciliation.

  • Improved cash flow: Faster payments and reduced manual processing contribute to smoother cash flow management.

Don't delay – register today on Embedded Virtual Card Payments for SAP Ariba Procurement and SAP Business Network using Taulia / https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#campaign/3707 and be a part of the SAP Customer Engagement Initiative Cycle 2 in 2024!

entry.title: Integrate Google BigQuery and SAP Ariba using SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Business Requirement:

Our business requirement was to post Non-PO Invoices from Google Big Query to SAP Ariba as cXML.

For our project, the BigQuery Connector solution wasn’t feasible due to constraints with SAP SLT. Instead, we leveraged an iFlow to integrate with Google Cloud Platform REST APIs. This allowed us to efficiently map the retrieved data to cXML format for seamless integration with SAP Ariba.

Also, I haven’t delved into the exception handling aspect yet. However, it could be straightforward, depending on the available applications such as BTP Alert Notification or just by implementing an exception subprocess.

Systems involved:

  • Google Big Query
  • SAP Ariba Buying & Invoicing
  • SAP Integration Suite

Scenario Description:

1 - The first step for integrating with Google BigQuery is to get the oAuth 2.0 credentials for the Google APIs. For doing that, we can follow the steps in their documentation:

https://support.google.com/googleapi/answer/6158849?hl=en&ref_topic=7013279

One important thing is that we will need to add our integration suite callback URL to the credentials configuration in the Authorized redirect URIs section. The URL will look like this:

https://{your_tenant_id}.integrationsuite.cfapps.{tenant_region}.hana.ondemand.com/itspaces/odata/ap...

We will need to replace {your_tenant_id} with our tenant id and the {tenant_region} with the region of our tenant (us10, us30, eu10…).

2 - After the credential is created, go to SAP Integration Suite Manage Security Material and create a credential with the type OAuth2 Authorization Code

Inform our newly created credential data and pass “https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud-platform” to the “Scope” field, then click on deploy.

3 - Before we can use our credential, we need to generate the first Authorization code for it. Click on “Authorize” in the menu for the entry we just created and follow the instructions on the screen. We will need to inform some Google credentials before finishing this step

4 - Unfortunately, the http receiver adapter does not support Authorization Code as an authentication method, so we have to get the code from the credential and inform it in a header field in my request. Here is the code for accomplishing that:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;
import com.sap.it.api.securestore.SecureStoreService;
import com.sap.it.api.securestore.AccessTokenAndUser;
import com.sap.it.api.securestore.exception.SecureStoreException;
import com.sap.it.api.ITApiFactory;
def Message processData(Message message) {
     
    SecureStoreService secureStoreService = ITApiFactory.getService(SecureStoreService.class, null)
 
    //Get token from credential named GCP
    AccessTokenAndUser accessTokenAndUser = secureStoreService.getAccesTokenForOauth2AuthorizationCodeCredential("GCP")
    String token = accessTokenAndUser.getAccessToken()
        
    message.setHeader("Authorization", "Bearer "+token)
    
   return message;
}

5 - The next step is to get the data from the BigQuery table. For that, we will use  the “jobs.query” method from the Google BigQuery API. Using this method allows us to send SQL statements to BigQuery directly. We can see the documentation for that method here:

https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/rest/v2/jobs/query

Since this is a pretty straight forward scenario, we can  just  generate the full request using a content modifier:

6 - After that, we will trigger the first integration. Keep in mind that we will need to set Authentication as “None” and inform “*” in the Request Headers field so the adapter will keep the “Authorization” header field that we created in our groovy script during the previous step

7 - The Google BigQuery API response for this call will be a JSON with several elements. The important ones for us at this moment are “schema” which contains the field specifications from our table  and “rows” which contains all the data that resulted from our query.

Note that the “rows” element contain only elements of the type “value”, so the response JSON should look like this:

{
	"schema": {
		"fields": [
			{
				"name": "invoice_id",
				"type": "STRING",
				"mode": "NULLABLE"
			},
			{
				"name": "line_item_number",
				"type": "INTEGER",
				"mode": "NULLABLE"
			},
			{
				"name": "date",
				"type": "STRING",
				"mode": "NULLABLE"
			},
			{
				"name": "line_item_amount",
				"type": "STRING",
				"mode": "NULLABLE"
			}
		]
	},
	"rows": [
		{
			"f": [
				{
					"v": "019845"
				},
				{
					"v": 1
				},
				{
					"v": "2024-06-12"
				},
				{
					"v": "954.21"
				}
			]
		},
		{
			"f": [
				{
					"v": "019845"
				},
				{
					"v": 2
				},
				{
					"v": "2024-06-12"
				},
				{
					"v": "4.33"
				}
			]
		}
	]
}

Since we need to map the data from the “rows” element to the Ariba cXML and we have the fields specification in the “schema” element, we can use this simple groovy script for assigning the field names to the rows we have:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message

def Message processData(Message message) {

    def jsonIn = message.getBody(String.class)
    def data = new groovy.json.JsonSlurper().parseText(jsonIn)
    def fieldNames = data.schema.fields.collect { it.name[0..-1] } 

    def writer = new StringWriter()
    def output = new groovy.xml.MarkupBuilder(writer)

    output.rows{
        data.rows.f.each { item ->
            row{
                item.v.eachWithIndex { fieldValue, index ->
                    def fieldname = fieldNames[index]
                    "$fieldname"(fieldValue) 
                }
            }
        }
    }

    message.setBody(writer.toString())
    return message
}

The output will look like this:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8">
<rows>
  <row>
    <invoice_id>019845</invoice_id>
    <line_item_number>1</line_item_number>
    <date>2024-06-12</date>
    <line_item_amount>954.21</line_item_amount>
  </row>
  <row>
    <invoice_id>019845</invoice_id>
    <line_item_number>2</line_item_number>
    <date>2024-06-12</date>
    <line_item_amount>4.33</line_item_amount>
  </row>
</rows>

8 - In our architecture, we can have more than one entry in the Google BigQuery table that is related to the same entity. eg One invoice may have multiple items and each item will generate one entry in the Google BigQuery table, so we need to group the entries by invoiceId. This is achievable by using a xslt transformation, but for me it is easier to do using groovy, so this is what I came up with:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import java.util.HashMap
import groovy.util.XmlParser
import groovy.xml.MarkupBuilder

def Message processData(Message message) {

    def body = message.getBody(java.lang.String);
    def parser = new XmlParser()
    def writer = new StringWriter()
    def builder = new MarkupBuilder(writer)
    def pXml1 = parser.parseText(body)
    def pXml2 = parser.parseText(body)
    def line_item = 0

    builder.rows{ 
        pXml1.row.each{ rowXml1 ->
            if(rowXml1.line_item_number.text() == '1'){
                row{
                    rowXml1.each{ element1 ->
                        fieldname1 = element1.name()
                            if (!fieldname1.startsWith("line_item")){
                                "$fieldname1"(element1.text())
                            }
                    }
                    line_item = 0
                    pXml2.row.each{ rowXml2 ->
                        if(rowXml2.date.text() == rowXml1.date.text() && rowXml2.invoice_id.text() == rowXml1.invoice_id.text()){    
                            item{
                                rowXml2.each { element2 ->
                                    fieldname2 = element2.name()
                                    if (fieldname2.startsWith("line_item")){
                                            "$fieldname2"(element2.text())
                                    }
                                }
                            }
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }			
    message.setBody(writer.toString())
    return message;
}

So we'll have an output like this:

<rows>
  <row>
    <invoice_id>019845</invoice_id>
    <date>2024-06-12</date>
    <item>
      <line_item_number>1</line_item_number>
      <line_item_amount>954.21</line_item_amount>
    </item>
    <item>
      <line_item_number>2</line_item_number>
      <line_item_amount>4.33</line_item_amount>
    </item>
  </row>
</rows>

9 - After all rows are grouped, we need to map invoice by invoice to the cXML format and then send the invoices to Ariba. So we can implement a General Splitter that will generate one message for each “row” tag in our XML:

10 - We need to capture the key tags from our document so we can easily update our BigQuery table after the process is finished. Also, in this case, we stored the PO number due to a business requirement validation

11 - The business requirement in this case is to post only the invoices that does not have a purchase order, so we validate the information we just stored in the previous step

For the cases where the invoice has a PO, we just set the ProcessFlag field in BigQuery as “Ignored” and the process is finished. So we used the same BigQuery API method and just changed the SQL statement:

13 - For the cases where the invoice doesn’t have a PO, we need to map the data to the InvoiceDetailRequest cXML schema. For doing that, first we need to download the schema from the URL below:

http://xml.cxml.org/schemas/cXML/1.2.060/InvoiceDetail.dtd

The file we downloaded has the “.dtd” extension and is not a valid schema file for our iFlow. The easiest way I found for solving this problem was to open the file using Microsoft Visual Studio and then go to: 

XML -> Create schema 

It will generate a xsd file based on the dtd, so we just need to adjust any errors later if necessary

After importing the generated xsd into our iFlow, we need to map all fields from our BigQuery invoice to the corresponding field in the XML schema:

14 - In the next step, we remove all namespaces and prefixes from the XML file using a xsl transformation:

<?xml version="1.0"?>
<xsl:stylesheet version="1.0" xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform">

    <xsl:output indent="yes" method="xml" encoding="utf-8" omit-xml-declaration="yes"/>

    <!-- Stylesheet to remove all namespaces from a document -->
    <!-- NOTE: this will lead to attribute name clash, if an element contains
        two attributes with same local name but different namespace prefix -->
    <!-- Nodes that cannot have a namespace are copied as such -->

    <!-- template to copy elements -->
    <xsl:template match="*">
        <xsl:element name="{local-name()}">
            <xsl:apply-templates select="@* | node()"/>
        </xsl:element>
    </xsl:template>

    <!-- template to copy attributes -->
    <xsl:template match="@*">
        <xsl:attribute name="{local-name()}">
            <xsl:value-of select="."/>
        </xsl:attribute>
    </xsl:template>

    <!-- template to copy the rest of the nodes -->
    <xsl:template match="comment() | text() | processing-instruction()">
        <xsl:copy/>
    </xsl:template>

</xsl:stylesheet>

With our main message mapped, the next step is to add the header fields. For that we can create some configurable exchange properties so we can change the data from test to production when we transport our solution:

15 - The groovy script below will generate the header tags of the cXML request, load them with the data from the configurable exchange properties we created in the previous step and then append our mapped XML to the “Request” element of the cXML:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import java.util.HashMap
import groovy.xml.MarkupBuilder
import groovy.xml.XmlUtil

def Message processData(Message message) {
    
    //Get message body as a string for appending to the xml document we'll generate here
    def body = message.getBody(java.lang.String)
    
    //Get exchange properties from message
    def supplierANID = message.getProperty("SupplierANID")
    def myANID = message.getProperty("MyANID")
    def mySharedSecret = message.getProperty("SharedSecret")
    def myDeploymentMode = message.getProperty("DeploymentMode")

    // Get unix timestamp for the payloadID
	def date = new Date();
    def unixTimestamp = date.getTime() / 1000

    //Generate a random number for the payloadID
    Random random = new Random()
    def randomNumber = random.nextInt(9999999)

    //Get current date for the "timestamp" field
    def currentDate = date.format("yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss")

    // Build XML header 
    def sw = new StringWriter()
    def cXml = new MarkupBuilder(sw)

    cXml.cXML(payloadID: unixTimestamp + "." + randomNumber + "@myCompany.com", timestamp: currentDate + "-01:00", version: "1.2.060") {
        Header {
            From {
                Credential(domain: "NetworkID") {
                    Identity(supplierANID)
                }
            }
            To {
                Credential(domain: "NetworkID") {
                    Identity(myANID)
                }
                Credential(domain: "SystemID") {
                    Identity("CHILD1")
                }
                Credential(domain: "EndPointID") {
                    Identity("Ariba")
                }
            }
            Sender {
                Credential(domain: "NetworkID") {
                    Identity("CHILD1-"+MyANID)
                    SharedSecret(mySharedSecret)
                }
                UserAgent("Google DocAI")
            }
    }
    Request(deploymentMode: myDeploymentMode) {
        //Append current message body to our xml document
        mkp.yieldUnescaped body
    }
}
    //All "lang" attributes must have the "xml:" prefix
	message.setBody(sw.toString().replaceAll("lang=", "xml:lang="))

    return message;
}

16 - After that, the last step before posting the file to Ariba is to add the required DOCTYPE declaration to our cXML message. For that, we can use this simple xsl transformation:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<xsl:stylesheet version="1.0"
                xmlns:xsl="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Transform"
                xmlns:fo="http://www.w3.org/1999/XSL/Format"
				exclude-result-prefixes="fo">
	<xsl:output method="xml" doctype-system="http://xml.cxml.org/schemas/cXML/1.2.060/InvoiceDetail.dtd"/>
	<xsl:template match="/">
		<xsl:copy-of select="*"/>
	</xsl:template>
</xsl:stylesheet>

17 - With the full cXML generated, we can just post them to the Ariba endpoint:

18 - After posting the cXML, we have a groovy script that removes the DOCTYPE declaration from the cXML and then parse the result so we can update the BigQuery table:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import java.util.HashMap
import groovy.xml.MarkupBuilder
import groovy.xml.XmlUtil
import groovy.json.*

def Message processData(Message message) {

    def body = message.getBody(java.lang.String)
    
    //Remove the <!DOCTYPE tag so we can parse as a common XML
    body = body.replaceAll("(?i)<!DOCTYPE[^<>]*(?:<!ENTITY[^<>]*>[^<>]*)?>", "")
    
    def response = new XmlSlurper().parseText(body)
    def AribaMessage = response.'*'.Status.text()
    def code = response.'*'.Status.@code.text()
    
    if(code != "200"){
        AribaStatus = "Error"
    } else {
        AribaStatus = "Accepted"
    }
    
    AribaMessage = AribaMessage.replaceAll(System.getProperty("line.separator"), "")
    AribaMessage = AribaMessage.replaceAll("\"", "")
    
    //Store the response into exchange properties so we can use them later
    message.setProperty("AribaCode", code)
    message.setProperty("AribaMessage", AribaMessage)
    message.setProperty("AribaStatus", AribaStatus)

    message.setBody("")
    return message;
}

And after that we can generate the SQL statement that updates the BigQuery table using the exchange properties generated in our last groovy script

Conclusion:

This is an alternative approach to integrating Google BigQuery with SAP Ariba by using an iFlow in SAP Integration Suite. The combination of graphical mapping, groovy script and XSLT has simplified the manipulation and transformation of the data for our purposes. The techniques presented may be a flexible solution for automating the document posting to SAP Ariba.

entry.title: Join us in shaping the future of mobile procurement!
entry.content:
.

Dear Ariba Community,

Imagine you can:

  • Shop and order from your company’s catalog with a personal AI assistant.
  • Get notified on your smart watch when a contract or sourcing task is assigned to you
  • Approve and monitor requisitions from your employees. 

We would love to hear your feedback on features you need most for your business and also how we can support your rollout of the mobile application to your employees as quickly as possible.

Please use this link to learn more about this CEI and register until 11. July 2024. 

https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#campaign/3734

Hope to see you there!

entry.title: Getting Started with SAP Ariba Shopping App in 7 steps
entry.content:
Administration guide for SAP Ariba Shopping with interactive onboarding diagram

Hello Ariba community,

I would like to share with you the latest product update: The new SAP Ariba Shopping administration guide is now online!

The administration guide provides you with an interactive onboarding diagram with all required steps and roles for a successful rollout. This blog post helps you with additional screenshots and useful hints in completing the 7 administrative tasks in the administration guide. It was never easier to get started with SAP Ariba Shopping!

Before you start, check the following prerequisites:

You have a valid subscription for one of the following Ariba purchases:

  • SAP Ariba Buying or
  • SAP Ariba Buying and Invoicing

You have administration rights for the following systems:

  • SAP BTP Global Account of your Ariba purchase 
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services / Corporate IDP
  • SAP Ariba Developer Portal

Step 1: Subscribe to SAP Shopping App with SAP Booster

A SAP Booster is a set of guided interactive steps that enable you to select, configure, and consume services on BTP. Search for the SAP Ariba Shopping Booster to subscribe to the BTP application.

You have to start the Booster at the BTP global account of your SAP Ariba purchase to subscribe to SAP Ariba Shopping. You can access BTP global accounts with the SAP BTP cockpit: https://emea.cockpit.btp.cloud.sap

Your company can have multiple BTP global accounts. A global account represents the sales contract and the subscriptions of the purchased solutions.  If you don't find the BTP global account of your SAP Ariba purchase, ask your SAP contact person (CSP - Customer Success Partner) or create a support ticket. For more information, see Contacting Support.

Read this section in the administration guide. Click on start.

SAP BTP Global Account, tab Boosters

Check Prerequisites 

The Booster starts with checking the prerequisites:

SAP Booster: Check Prerequisites

If the authorization check fails, you probably have no global account administrator rights. If the entitlements check fails, the global account does not belong to the Ariba purchase. If you do not find the global account for the Arba purchase, contact your SAP contact or create a support ticket.

Click on Next when the checks are successful.  

Select Scenario

SAP Booster: Select Scenario

You can create or select from existing subaccounts. If you select an existing subaccount you may skip to configure the SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS) tenant because this step is probably already completed. For this blog post, I select the option to create a new subaccount. Click on Next.

Configure subaccount

Enter now the subaccount details. To minimize network latency, BTP data center and Ariba data center should be in the same region. Click on Next.

SAP Booster: Configure Subaccount

Select Identity provider for platform and application and add users

In this step you select the identity provider for the platform and the identity provider for the application. In a productive scenario you would select the identify provider which is used to identify Ariba end users. In a testing scenario you can select the identify provider where you are authorized to create users. 

Furthermore, you can add other colleagues (you are already selected per default) to subaccount administrator role and developer role.  The developer role is needed to access the SAP Mobile Service cockpit.

SAP Booster: Add Users

Review before automation starts

After reviewing the input, the Booster makes the following manual tasks for you. Click on Finish.

SAP Booster: Review

Step 2: Configure Identify Authentication Service

In this step you establish trust between SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS) of the application and the subaccount with the SAML 2.0 protocol. Read this section in the admin guide to complete on this common task.

To create test users, you assign the user to Purchasing User and Mobile Shopping user groups at SAP Ariba Buying (P2P). Ensure that the user groups are top-level user groups, and not child user groups. In the IAS you can use the Login attribute to identify the Ariba end user. Then you maintain the SAP Ariba User Id in the login attributes of the test user.

SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS) : Maintain Login Name as identifier (left), maintain Login Name (right)

Step 3: Create Application Definitions in SAP Ariba Developer Portal

Sign in to the SAP Ariba APIs developer portal:

SAP Ariba Developer Portal - Login screen

Use the recommended namings from this section in the administration guide and create four application definitions.

SAP Ariba Developer Portal: Example Application Definition

Note down the generated application keys. You do not have to request API access. The service request you will create in the next step 4 will process the next three steps in the process (see screenshot, "Whats next"-section)  

SAP Ariba Developer Portal: Approval workflow for application

Step 4: Create Service request to enable SAP Ariba Shopping APIs

Read this section in the admin guide. Download the the SAML XML file of the BTP destination to add the file as an attachment to the service request:

SAP BTP subaccount: Destinations

Create the service request (support component: BNS-ARI-SAS-API) in SAP for Me with the following content:

Dear SAP Support,

The information to enable the SAP Ariba APIs is as follows.

  • Site Name: The site name (realm) where you want to connect the app with

  • Data Center: The data center from the Ariba Developer Portal where you have created the four applications 

  • ANID: [Example: AN01037605217-T] - The technical id of the Ariba realm where you want to connect the app with

  • Application Keys:

    • SAS Procurement Requisition: [application key]

    • SAS Procurement Chooser: [application key]

    • SAS User Schema Information: [application key]

    • SAS Catalog Search: [application key]

Attached is the SAML XML metadata file from the SAP BTP subaccount.

The processing of the service request takes usually one or two working days. Once completed, you see in the developer portal that the applications are approved for the Ariba Shopping APIs. Request now the oAuth client secrets for the four applications from your Ariba administrator. Note down also the Oauth Client id for the four applications and the real name ("cirrusatlanticsuite" in my example) for later use in step 6.

SAP Ariba Developer Portal: application status after processed service request

Step 5: Connect SAP Mobile Services to SAP Ariba Shopping

Read this section in the administration guide. Select the subaccount in the SAP BTP cockpit. Click on SAP Ariba Shopping application:

SAP BTP cockpit: subaccount

The URL to access the SAP Mobile Service Cockpit is shown in the browser:

SAP BTP cockpit: output of SAP Ariba Shopping application

Copy and bookmark the SAP Mobile Service URL and note down the mobile Destination URL for the next step. Open now the SAP Mobile Service URL to access the SAP Mobile Service cockpit, Kernel Service.

The Kernel Service is used for an application like SAP Ariba Shopping that is managed in a single-app cockpit (Software as a Service, or SaaS). The license type is read-only and cannot be selected or edited in the SAP BTP cockpit. 

Go to tab "info", select feature "Mobile Connectivity":

SAP Mobile Service Cockpit: Start section with assigned features

You see three mobile destinations:

SAP Mobile Service Cockpit: Destinations to SAP Ariba

To configure the three destinations you need first obtain from SAP Ariba Developer Portal the following:

  • The value of the host of the Production & Test URL. Example: https://openapi.ariba.com. 
  • The value of the Token Service URL: Example: https://api.ariba.com/v2/oauth/token

SAP Ariba Developer Portal: Section Discover

Update all three destinations with the right values from the admin guide. Every destinations requires slightly different information. I selected the images destination as an example:

Basic Info

SAP Mobile Service cockpit: Enter Basis Info

Custom Headers

In the second step you enter the application key of the Ariba application "SAS Catalog Search":

SAP Mobile Service Cockpit: Custom Headers

Annotations

Click on Next.

Destination Configuration

Read the step in admin guide to update the fields with the right values.

SAP Mobile Service Cockpit: Destination configuration

Certificate Configuration

Click on Finish. When you have updated all three destinations you should see a similar result:

SAP Mobile Service cockpit: All destinations updated

Click on the row of the requisition-attachment to access the security section. Download SAML metadata file and set expiry date (maximum is 10 years). Update the created Service Report with the SAML metadata file to enable the API for the attachment functionality.

SAP Mobile Service Cockpit: Access security section of destination

Step 6: Configure SAP BTP Destination To SAP Ariba

Read this section in the admin guide. Go to the Destination section of the subaccount and update the four Destinations: 

BTP Cockpit: Update 4 destinations

Here is an example how I updated the first destination. The warning is not applicable for this step.

SAP cockpit: Example to update first destination

Check the connection for all four destinations. A green checkmark and a message that says the connection is established indicates that your configuration was successful. The returned response doesn't matter even if it is HTTP Status code 403 or 404.

 

Step 7: Onboard Users to SAP Ariba Shopping

Read this section in the admin guide. Go to SAP Mobile Service cockpit, navigate to API tab to view the QR code:

SAP Mobile Service Cockpit: API tab
 
  1. Download (iOS/Android) and open SAP Ariba Shopping app 
  2. Scan QR Code and check connection request
  3. Agree to end user license agreement (EULA)
  4. Start shopping!
SAP Ariba Shopping app on iOS

Further References:

  • Watch SAP Ariba Shopping app on Youtube.
  • Our roadmaps for iOS and Android.
  • Join our new Customer Engagement Initiative to design with us the future of Ariba mobile.
  • Blog post from my colleague Jens Trummer about the first release

entry.title: Enhanced Continuous Quality Check for SAP Ariba and SAP Business Network Now Available
entry.content:
“Continuous quality checks” are individual services delivered by SAP Enterprise Support and are available with every cloud subscription. The remote-service reports help you reduce technical risks and identify areas for optimization potential, based on real-life data from your systems or solution.

The revamped Continuous Quality Check (CQC) for deployment readiness for SAP Ariba and SAP Business Network is now available. This CQC is designed to provide an SAP best practice review for key settings in your SAP Ariba and SAP Business Network instance.

View the CQC info sheet for full details.

Solutions Covered by This Service

  • SAP Ariba Sourcing
  • SAP Ariba Contracts
  • SAP Ariba Supplier Lifecycle and Performance
  • SAP Ariba Supplier Risk NEW
  • SAP Ariba Buying
  • SAP Ariba Buying and Invoicing
  • SAP Ariba Catalog
  • SAP Ariba Invoice Management
  • SAP Business Network for Procurement
  • SAP Business Network Commerce Automation NEW
  • SAP Business Network Supply Chain Collaboration NEW
  • SAP Integration Suite, managed gateway for spend management and SAP Business Network NEW

Key Benefits

  • Review current settings, use of features, and enablement of key functions.
  • Identify concerns, issues and potential risks prior to deployment/go-live.
  • Receive a detailed service report that highlights key areas for consideration and SAP best practice recommendations.
  • Optimize the possibility for a smooth start of production.

When to Use

  • Before the go-live of a new SAP Ariba implementation.
  • When additional solutions are added to the existing SAP Ariba suite.
  • As a "health check" for productive SAP Ariba and SAP Business Network systems.

How to Request

  • Remote Services can be requested via the new Get Support application in SAP for Me (SAP Note 1296527). When submitting your request, please select:
  1. Product: “Continuous Quality Check & Improvement Services”
  2. Product function: “Service request for a Cloud Solution”
  • If you need assistance in submitting your case, you may contact your local Customer Interaction Center (CIC).
  • SAP recommends service booking of six (6) weeks prior to go-live date, to allow for the delivery four (4) weeks before your go-live date and provide adequate time to review and implement any SAP recommendations (attempts will be made to accommodate requests made outside this timeframe, but are not guaranteed).
  • SAP will evaluate if this service is suitable for your business needs.
  • SAP recommends the service to be performed on the productive tenant, if not available it can be performed on the test tenant.

Achieve More with SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps

Find the right content to accelerate the value of your solution with the Spend Management and Business Network value map from SAP Enterprise Support. Included with every SAP cloud subscription, value maps offer structured, product-level Learning Rooms where you can navigate to curated content, be directed to self-service platforms, engage with fellow customers and SAP experts, and discover continuous quality checks to mitigate implementation risks or identify optimization potential.

Explore how SAP technology can transform your procurement operation or make your existing software work harder for you by visiting value maps today.

Access Value Maps in 3 Easy Steps:   

  1. One-time sign-up for SAP Learning Hub, edition for SAP Enterprise Support, is required   
  2. Access value maps 
  3. Navigate to the SAP Enterprise Support value map for Spend Management and Business Network 

Once in the value map, we welcome you to collaborate in our forums, ask questions or “Request a Call” to speak with one of our experts for further help. Value maps are easy to navigate but an on-boarding call allows us to tailor the conversation to suit your needs and highlight specific content for you. 

Got a question about value maps, or our content?

📺Watch our short video.

Leverage the “ask questions” feature in the value map or email us:
sapesvaluemaps@sap.com
